answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or repetitive activities involving your elbow?
answers.intake_a3: No injury, but I have been playing a lot of tennis and the pain started after my last game
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty or pain when you try to grip or lift objects with your hand?
answers.intake_q7: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or remedies for the elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_a8: icing on the first day of pain
answers.intake_q9: Any previous history of elbow problems or arthritis?
answers.intake_q10: Any pain at rest or only with activity?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began after playing tennis and is primarily activity-related, especially with lifting objects, without associated swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, or weakness. She denies any recent injury, systemic symptoms such as fever or chills, and has no prior history of elbow problems or arthritis. Initial self-treatment included icing on the first day of pain. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by activity and repetitive wrist extension movements common in tennis. The absence of systemic symptoms and neurological signs supports this diagnosis.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure appropriate dosing and duration to minimize adverse effects. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or fever, or if symptoms do not improve with conservative management over the next 4 to 6 weeks. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist or worsen despite treatment.

Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis | RA | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: joints. RA affects people differently. You might have the disease for only a short time, or your symptoms might come and go. The severe form can be chronic (long-lasting) and may last a lifetime. Symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis may include: Joint pain or stiffness when moving. This is usually worse in the morning or after inactivity. RA usually affects both…
